seo.title: Cybersecurity Research Agents AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for research agents AI agents in cybersecurity. Covers NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001.

content.intro: Deploying AI research agents in cybersecurity environments introduces unique risks where leaked threat intelligence, hallucinated CVE data, or unauthorized automated responses can directly empower adversaries or trigger catastrophic outages. This checklist addresses the intersection of NIST CSF, SOC 2 Type II, and ISO 27001 requirements with the specific operational patterns of multi-step web search and synthesis agents. Following these controls is essential to ensure AI-assisted threat research accelerates your security posture without becoming an attack surface itself.

sections.heading: Data Handling & Intelligence Confidentiality
sections.description: Prevent threat intelligence, IOCs, and classified security data from being exfiltrated through LLM API calls, web search queries, or synthesis outputs sent to third-party services.

items.label: Sanitize inputs before LLM API transmission
items.description: Strip or redact all internal IOCs, IP ranges, CVE exploit details, and proprietary threat actor TTPs from prompts before sending to any external LLM API endpoint. Implement a pre-processing filter that classifies data sensitivity using a local model or regex ruleset.

items.label: Enforce data residency for classified threat data
items.description: Ensure that any agent processing classified or restricted threat intelligence (e.g., ISAC feeds, government advisories) routes exclusively through on-premises or private cloud LLM deployments, never through shared public API endpoints.

items.label: Log all outbound agent queries with DLP scanning
items.description: Integrate a Data Loss Prevention (DLP) layer that inspects every web search query and API call generated by the research agent for sensitive keywords, internal hostnames, vulnerability details, and threat actor identifiers before transmission.

items.label: Implement output classification tagging
items.description: Automatically tag all AI-generated research summaries with a classification level (e.g., TLP:RED, TLP:AMBER) based on the sensitivity of source data consumed, and enforce access controls on downstream distribution of those outputs.

items.label: Restrict agent memory and context persistence
items.description: Disable persistent memory or long-term context storage in research agents that process sensitive threat data. Ensure each session is ephemeral and that no threat intelligence is retained in agent state between research tasks.

items.label: Audit third-party LLM provider data retention policies
items.description: Confirm in writing that any third-party LLM API provider used by the research agent does not retain, train on, or log prompt content. Obtain contractual data processing agreements aligned with ISO 27001 Annex A.13 controls.

items.label: Isolate agent network egress to approved domains only
items.description: Enforce network-level allowlisting so the research agent can only perform web searches and API calls to pre-approved domains (e.g., NVD, MITRE ATT&CK, VirusTotal). Block all other egress to prevent unintended data exfiltration channels.

sections.heading: Source Verification & Claim Accuracy
sections.description: Ensure all AI-synthesized security findings, CVE references, and threat advisories are grounded in verified, authoritative sources before being acted upon or distributed to security teams.

items.label: Cross-reference CVE claims against NVD and MITRE databases
items.description: Implement an automated post-processing step that validates every CVE identifier mentioned in agent outputs against the NIST National Vulnerability Database (NVD) API and MITRE CVE list, flagging any CVE that does not exist or has mismatched severity scores.

items.label: Enforce source provenance tracking for all research outputs
items.description: Require the agent to cite the specific URL, publication date, and author for every factual claim in its research summaries. Implement a provenance metadata schema that is stored alongside each output for audit purposes.

items.label: Implement recency validation for threat intelligence
items.description: Automatically reject or flag any source older than a configurable threshold (default: 90 days for active threat data, 12 months for TTPs) and require the agent to surface the publication date prominently in all outputs.

items.label: Deploy hallucination detection for security-specific claims
items.description: Run a secondary verification pass using a separate model or rule-based checker that identifies statistically improbable claims, non-existent CVE numbers, fabricated threat actor names, or tool capabilities not corroborated by at least two independent sources.

items.label: Validate MITRE ATT&CK technique mappings
items.description: When the agent maps observed behaviors to ATT&CK techniques, automatically verify that the cited technique ID and name match the current ATT&CK version via the MITRE ATT&CK STIX API, and flag deprecated or renamed techniques.

items.label: Flag single-source claims for human review
items.description: Automatically route any security advisory or threat claim supported by only a single source to a human analyst queue before it is included in incident reports or distributed to stakeholders.

items.label: Detect and label potential bias in threat actor attribution
items.description: Apply a bias detection check on threat actor attribution claims, flagging outputs that rely exclusively on vendor-sponsored reports or geopolitically motivated sources without corroboration from neutral parties such as government CERTs or academic researchers.

sections.heading: Human-in-the-Loop Controls for Automated Actions
sections.description: Enforce mandatory human approval gates for any agent-recommended or agent-initiated actions that could affect production systems, incident response workflows, or security tool configurations.

items.label: Require explicit human approval for all critical system response actions
items.description: Implement a hard-coded approval workflow that blocks the agent from executing any action classified as critical (e.g., firewall rule changes, endpoint isolation, account lockouts, SIEM rule deployment) until a named security analyst approves via a signed authorization token.

items.label: Define and enforce agent action permission tiers
items.description: Create a tiered permission model: Tier 1 (read-only research, no approval needed), Tier 2 (low-impact actions like tagging alerts, require single approval), Tier 3 (critical system changes, require dual authorization from senior analyst and CISO or delegate).

items.label: Implement a mandatory dry-run mode for new agent playbooks
items.description: Require all new or modified agent response playbooks to execute in simulation mode for a minimum of 5 business days, logging intended actions without executing them, before being approved for live operation.

items.label: Set automated action rate limits and circuit breakers
items.description: Configure rate limits on agent-initiated actions (e.g., maximum 10 automated low-tier actions per hour) and implement circuit breakers that halt all agent actions and alert the SOC if anomalous action volumes are detected.

items.label: Maintain a tamper-evident audit log of all agent actions
items.description: Log every agent action, recommendation, and approval decision to an append-only, cryptographically signed audit trail stored in a system the agent cannot modify, aligned with SOC 2 Type II CC7.2 and ISO 27001 A.12.4 logging requirements.

items.label: Define explicit rollback procedures for each automated action type
items.description: For every action the agent is permitted to take, document and test a rollback procedure. Ensure the agent can automatically initiate rollback for Tier 2 actions and that Tier 3 rollbacks require human execution with a documented runbook.

items.label: Notify affected system owners before agent-initiated changes
items.description: Implement pre-action notifications to system owners and on-call engineers at least 5 minutes before any agent-initiated change to production systems, with a cancellation window, except in confirmed active-breach scenarios with CISO override.

sections.heading: Compliance Monitoring & Incident Response Readiness
sections.description: Establish continuous compliance monitoring, regular audits, and a tested incident response plan specifically covering AI agent failure modes including adversarial prompt injection, data leakage, and false positive escalations.

items.label: Conduct quarterly red team exercises targeting agent prompt injection
items.description: Perform structured adversarial testing every quarter where a red team attempts to manipulate the research agent via crafted web content, malicious search results, or injected instructions in scraped pages to exfiltrate data or trigger unauthorized actions.

items.label: Map agent controls to NIST CSF functions in a living document
items.description: Maintain a control mapping document that explicitly links each agent safeguard to NIST CSF Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover functions, updated with every agent version release and reviewed during annual ISO 27001 audits.

items.label: Define AI-specific incident response runbooks
items.description: Create and test dedicated incident response runbooks for AI agent failure scenarios: (1) confirmed intelligence leakage via LLM API, (2) agent triggering false positive mass-isolation event, (3) adversarial manipulation of agent outputs detected. Each runbook must include containment, eradication, and recovery steps.

items.label: Implement continuous drift detection on agent behavior baselines
items.description: Establish behavioral baselines for the research agent (query volume, source diversity, output length, action frequency) and deploy anomaly detection that alerts the SOC when agent behavior deviates more than two standard deviations from baseline, indicating potential compromise or misconfiguration.

items.label: Perform SOC 2 Type II evidence collection for agent controls
items.description: Designate a compliance owner responsible for collecting and organizing SOC 2 Type II audit evidence specific to AI agent controls, including access logs, approval records, DLP scan results, and training completion records, on a continuous basis rather than point-in-time.

items.label: Align agent deployment with CISA AI security guidance and directives
items.description: Review all applicable CISA directives and CISA AI security guidance documents (including CISA's Guidelines for Secure AI System Development) at each agent version release and document any required control adjustments before deployment to production.

items.label: Establish a vulnerability disclosure process for agent-specific flaws
items.description: Create an internal responsible disclosure process for reporting and remediating vulnerabilities discovered in the AI research agent itself, including prompt injection vectors, data leakage paths, and logic flaws, with SLA targets of 24 hours for critical, 7 days for high severity.

items.label: Train SOC analysts on AI agent limitations and failure modes
items.description: Deliver mandatory training to all SOC analysts who consume agent outputs covering: LLM hallucination risks, how to verify AI-generated CVE data, recognizing signs of prompt injection in agent outputs, and the escalation path when agent behavior appears anomalous. Require annual recertification.

notes.note: Research agents must be mapped to all six CSF functions (Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover). The new Govern function specifically requires organizations to establish policies for AI-assisted security tools, including defined roles, risk tolerance thresholds for automated actions, and supply chain risk management for LLM API providers used by the agent.

Under Common Criteria CC6 (Logical and Physical Access) and CC7 (System Operations), auditors will scrutinize whether AI research agents have appropriate access controls preventing them from reading or transmitting data beyond their defined scope, and whether anomaly detection covers AI-generated actions. Ensure agent service accounts follow least-privilege principles and that all agent-initiated system changes appear in monitored change logs reviewed during the audit period.

ISO 27001:2022 introduced controls specifically addressing secure development (A.8.25) and information security for use of cloud services (A.5.23), both directly applicable to AI research agents. Organizations must document the security requirements for the agent's development lifecycle, conduct threat modeling specific to the agent architecture, and formally assess the risk of each third-party LLM or search API used as part of the agent's tool chain.

CISA's 2024 Guidelines for Secure AI System Development (co-sealed with international partners) require that AI systems used in critical infrastructure security contexts implement secure-by-design principles, including input validation against prompt injection, output filtering before action execution, and transparency mechanisms so human operators can understand and override agent decisions. CISA BODs (Binding Operational Directives) for federal agencies additionally require that any AI tool processing threat intelligence be inventoried in the agency's software asset management system and assessed under the agency's ATO process.

cta.heading: Automate Your Cybersecurity Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI research agents against NIST CSF, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 controls—automatically flagging compliance gaps, logging human approval decisions, and generating audit-ready evidence before your next assessment.
